m03.title: Lab 3 — Your First Vibe Coding Projects
m03.objective: Experience AI-assisted development from scratch: start with a visual mockup and then build a complete full-stack application.

exercises.title: Visual mockup: Todo List App
exercises.instructions:
Open a new Copilot Chat (agent mode) and type this prompt:

> "Create a beautiful, modern Todo List web application using only HTML, CSS and vanilla JavaScript in a single file. It should have: a text input to add tasks, a list that shows tasks with a checkbox to mark as done (with strikethrough effect), a delete button per task, a counter showing pending tasks, and a clean dark-themed design with rounded corners and smooth animations."

Once generated, open the HTML file in your browser and test it.

Then iterate! Try these follow-up prompts:
- "Add categories (Work, Personal, Urgent) with colored labels"
- "Add local storage so tasks persist after refresh"
- "Add a filter bar: All / Active / Completed"
exercises.validation: You should have a fully functional todo app running in the browser with add, complete, and delete functionality. The key learning: a single prompt can produce a complete, visually polished app.

exercises.title: Full-stack: Contact Manager API
exercises.instructions:
Start a new project. Use this prompt:

> "Create a Python application with FastAPI that exposes a REST API to manage a contact list (name, email, phone). Include endpoints to create, list, update and delete contacts. Store data in memory using a list. Include a frontend in HTML that consumes that API — a clean, single-page interface with a form to add contacts and a table that shows all contacts with edit and delete buttons. Serve the HTML from FastAPI itself."

Once generated:
1. Install dependencies: pip install fastapi uvicorn
2. Run the server: python main.py
3. Open http://localhost:8000 in your browser
4. Test the full CRUD: add, edit and delete contacts

Then iterate:
- "Add input validation: email must have @ and phone must be numeric"
- "Add a search bar that filters contacts in real time"
exercises.validation: A running FastAPI server with 4 working endpoints (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and an HTML frontend that allows full CRUD operations on contacts through the browser.

exercises.title: Iterate and improve
exercises.instructions:
Take the contact manager from exercise 3.2 and improve it with these prompts:

> "Add a button to export all contacts as a CSV file"

> "Add a dark/light theme toggle"

> "Add a confirmation dialog before deleting a contact"

Observe how the AI understands the existing code context and adds features incrementally without breaking what already works.

Reflection: How many lines of code did the AI generate vs. how many you wrote manually?
exercises.validation: The contact app should now have export, theme toggle, and delete confirmation features. You experienced the iterative vibe coding workflow.

m05.title: Lab 5 — GitFlow & CI/CD with AI
m05.objective: Set up a professional branching strategy and let AI generate your CI/CD pipeline.

exercises.title: Set up GitFlow branches
exercises.instructions:
1. Create the branch structure:
```
git branch develop
git checkout -b feature/user-auth develop
```
2. Ask AI to implement the feature in the feature branch
3. When done, merge back to develop:
```
git checkout develop
git merge --no-ff feature/user-auth
```

Observe the merge commit in `git log --graph`.
exercises.validation: You should have main, develop and at least one merged feature branch visible in git log --graph.

exercises.title: AI-generated CI/CD
exercises.instructions:
Ask Cascade:

> "Generate a GitHub Actions workflow that: runs linting (ruff), runs tests (pytest), builds a Docker image, and deploys to a staging environment on push to develop. Use Python 3.11."

Review the generated `.github/workflows/ci.yml`. Does it follow best practices?
exercises.validation: The workflow file should have proper structure with jobs, steps, proper Python setup and test execution.

m09.title: Lab 9 — Prompt Engineering: From Idea to Project
m09.objective: Build structured prompts following the Module 7 template (Declaration, Functional Description, Tech Stack, Technical Guidelines) to kick off real projects with AI.

exercises.title: Structured prompt: Recipe App
exercises.instructions:
Build a complete prompt following the 4 sections from the Module 7 template for a cooking recipe app.

Your prompt must cover:

1. **Project Declaration**: name and application type
2. **Functional Description**: what the user can do (search recipes, filter by ingredients, save favorites, view prep time)
3. **Tech Stack**: choose and justify (e.g. Frontend: React + Tailwind, Backend: FastAPI, DB: SQLite)
4. **Technical Guidelines**: exclusions, API format, folder structure, error handling

Send the prompt to the AI and evaluate:
- Did it respect your stack?
- Does the folder structure match?
- Did it use any library you excluded?
exercises.starter_code:
# Base template — fill in with your decisions:

# 1. PROJECT DECLARATION
# Starting new project: [NAME]
# It will be a [application type]

# 2. FUNCTIONAL DESCRIPTION
# - The application must allow [main functionality]
# - Users can [key actions]
# - Main entities: [list them]

# 3. TECH STACK
# - Frontend: [framework + CSS]
# - Backend: [language + framework]
# - Database: [engine]

# 4. TECHNICAL GUIDELINES
# - DO NOT use [exclusions]
# - API format: [REST JSON / GraphQL]
# - Structure: [architectural pattern]
# - Error handling: [strategy]
exercises.validation: The prompt must have all 4 sections complete. The AI must generate code that exactly respects the stack and exclusions specified.

exercises.title: Structured prompt: Room Booking System
exercises.instructions:
Build a structured prompt for a meeting room booking system with more complex requirements.

Follow the Module 7 template with these constraints:

1. **Declaration**: REST API for room booking management
2. **Functional Description**:
   - Room CRUD (name, capacity, equipment)
   - Create bookings with overlap validation
   - View availability by day/week
   - Cancel bookings with 24h advance policy
3. **Stack**: Python 3.11 + FastAPI + SQLAlchemy + SQLite. DO NOT use Django or Flask.
4. **Technical Guidelines**:
   - Clean Architecture (domain, application, infrastructure)
   - API responses in {data, error, meta} format
   - Tests with pytest
   - Error handling with custom domain exceptions

Send to AI and verify that:
- It did NOT use Django or Flask
- Layers are properly separated
- Overlap validation is real, not a placeholder
exercises.validation: The AI must generate a Clean Architecture structure with layer separation, use FastAPI + SQLAlchemy (no Django/Flask), and implement real time-slot overlap validation.

exercises.title: Compare: vague prompt vs. structured prompt
exercises.instructions:
This exercise demonstrates why the 4 sections from Module 7 matter.

**Step 1** — Send this VAGUE prompt to AI:
> "Build me an app to manage a product inventory"

Note what stack it chose, what structure it generated, and what it assumed on its own.

**Step 2** — Now send the STRUCTURED prompt:
> "Starting new project: InventoryPro.
> It will be a REST API with SPA frontend.
>
> Functional description:
> - Product CRUD (name, SKU, price, stock)
> - Alerts when stock drops below a configurable threshold
> - Stock movement history (in/out with reason)
> - Dashboard with products below minimum stock
>
> Technologies:
> - Frontend: Vue 3 + Composition API + PicoCSS
> - Backend: Python FastAPI
> - Database: SQLite for development
>
> Technical guidelines:
> - DO NOT use React or Angular
> - API responses in format: {success: bool, data: any, error: string|null}
> - Start by creating models and endpoints, then the frontend
> - Error handling: HTTPException with specific codes"

**Step 3** — Compare both responses side by side:
- Which one respected your intent more?
- How many corrections did each need?
- Which generated more directly usable code?
exercises.validation: You must document the concrete differences between both responses: chosen stack, structure, incorrect assumptions from the vague prompt vs. precision of the structured one.

exercises.title: Iterative prompt: extend an existing project
exercises.instructions:
Use the project generated in exercise 9.2 (Room Booking System) and extend it with follow-up prompts that maintain consistency with the original stack and architecture.

Build 3 extension prompts following the same structure:

**Extension 1 — Notifications:**
> "Following the same Clean Architecture of the project, add an email notification system for 15 minutes before a booking. Create the use case in the application layer, the notifier interface in domain, and the SMTP implementation in infrastructure."

**Extension 2 — Reports:**
> "Add a GET /api/reports/usage endpoint that returns usage statistics per room (total bookings, occupied hours, cancellation rate). Keep the {data, error, meta} response format. Include date range filters."

**Extension 3 — Authentication:**
> "Add JWT authentication. Users must register and log in to create bookings. Only admin role users can create rooms. Maintain layer separation: JWT goes in infrastructure, roles in domain."

Verify that in each extension the AI maintains:
- The same stack (FastAPI + SQLAlchemy)
- The same folder structure
- The same API response format
- The same naming conventions
exercises.validation: All 3 extensions must maintain total consistency with the original project: same stack, same layer structure, same API format. The AI must not switch frameworks or break the existing architecture.

m12.title: Lab 12 — Context Injection Mastery
m12.objective: Practice advanced context injection techniques for precise AI responses.

exercises.title: Context chunking
exercises.instructions:
Practice context injection with a large codebase simulation:

1. Create a file header comment: `// File: src/services/order.service.ts`
2. Provide ONLY the relevant service to AI, not the entire project
3. Include the interface/type definitions it depends on
4. Ask for a specific modification

Key: minimum context, maximum precision.
exercises.starter_code:
# Simulate context injection:

# 1. Relevant type definition:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class OrderStatus(Enum):
    PENDING = "pending"
    CONFIRMED = "confirmed"
    SHIPPED = "shipped"
    DELIVERED = "delivered"

@dataclass
class Order:
    id: int
    customer_id: int
    status: OrderStatus
    total: float

# 2. Current service (the one to modify):
class OrderService:
    def __init__(self, repository):
        self.repository = repository
    
    def create_order(self, customer_id: int, items: list) -> Order:
        # existing implementation
        pass

# 3. Ask AI: "Add a cancel_order method that only allows cancellation if status is PENDING"
exercises.validation: The AI should use the exact OrderStatus enum and respect the existing code style.

exercises.title: Error log analysis
exercises.instructions:
Pass this real-looking error log to AI:

> "Analyze this error log. Identify the root cause, the affected file and line, and propose a fix."

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/app/src/services/payment.py", line 45, in process_payment
    result = gateway.charge(amount=order.total, currency="USD")
  File "/app/src/infrastructure/stripe_gateway.py", line 23, in charge
    response = self.client.charges.create(amount=int(amount * 100))
  File "/app/venv/lib/stripe/api.py", line 89, in create
    raise InvalidRequestError("Amount must be at least $0.50")
stripe.error.InvalidRequestError: Amount must be at least $0.50
```
exercises.validation: AI should identify that orders with total < 0.50 cause the error and suggest input validation.

m15.title: Lab 15 — Capstone: End-to-End Solution
m15.objective: Design and build a complete solution using all vibe coding techniques learned.

exercises.title: Full solution design
exercises.instructions:
You are tasked with building a **Team Standup Bot** that:
- Collects daily standup updates from team members via a REST API
- Stores: what I did yesterday, what I'll do today, any blockers
- Generates a daily summary digest
- Sends notifications for unresolved blockers

Ask AI to:
1. Design the system architecture (present 2-3 options with trade-offs)
2. Select the best option and justify
3. Create a Mermaid diagram
4. Define the API contract (OpenAPI)
exercises.validation: Should have: architecture comparison, selected option with justification, Mermaid diagram, and OpenAPI spec.

exercises.title: Implement with all techniques
exercises.instructions:
Implement the Standup Bot using ALL techniques from the course:

1. ✅ Architecture prompt (Module 7)
2. ✅ Design first, then code (Module 8)
3. ✅ Tests first — TDD (Module 8)
4. ✅ CONTEXT.md for the project (Module 10)
5. ✅ Clean git history (Module 4)
6. ✅ Proper error handling (Module 9)

This is your chance to demonstrate the full vibe coding workflow!
exercises.starter_code:
# Start with the CONTEXT.md:

# CONTEXT.md
## Standup Bot API
Python 3.11 + FastAPI + SQLAlchemy + SQLite
Clean Architecture | Repository Pattern

## API Endpoints
- POST /api/standups — Submit a standup update
- GET /api/standups/today — Get today's updates
- GET /api/standups/summary — Get daily digest
- GET /api/blockers — Get unresolved blockers

# Then ask AI to implement following TDD...
exercises.validation: Complete working API with tests, proper architecture, clean commits and documentation.
